Title: Blocking the Metabolism of Starch Breakdown Products in Arabidopsis Leaves Triggers Chloroplast Degradation

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of MEX1 causes chloroplastic accumulation of maltose and starch, reduced chlorophyll, fewer chloroplasts in mature leaves, and autophagy-like chloroplast degradation.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major nocturnal export product of transitory starch breakdown supporting nighttime metabolism
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Chloroplast stroma/envelope-associated export step in leaves
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotyping of mex1; genetic interaction with pgm, dpe1, bam1, bam3, bam4, and gwd; starch/maltose/maltotriose quantification; chlorophyll measurements; chloroplast counting; transmission electron microscopy; microarray analysis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. mex1 mutants accumulated high levels of maltose and starch in chloroplasts and developed age-dependent chlorosis. 2. Chlorosis was suppressed by blocking starch synthesis or starch breakdown upstream of maltose production, and enhanced by combining mex1 with dpe1. 3. mex1 mature leaves had fewer than half the chloroplast number of wild type and showed autophagy-like chloroplast degradation, linking blocked maltose export to chloroplast homeostasis failure.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced growth, mature-leaf chlorosis, thinner leaves, reduced cell size, decreased photosynthetic efficiency, chloroplast loss.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 12-h and 16-h photoperiods; continuous low light and exogenous sugars improved growth but not chlorosis
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper’s central causal gene. MEX1 is the chloroplast envelope transporter for maltose export during the night. Microarray and ultrastructural data suggest chloroplast degradation is not due to senescence induction but is associated with malto-oligosaccharide accumulation.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Maintaining effective chloroplast maltose export is critical for preserving chloroplast integrity during transitory starch turnover; transporter defects can cause severe source-leaf dysfunction.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of BAM1 had no detectable effect on starch, maltose, or chlorophyll in the mex1 background under the conditions tested.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major nocturnal export product of transitory starch breakdown supporting nighttime metabolism
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant combination analysis (bam1/mex1); starch, maltose, and chlorophyll measurements
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BAM1 is described as an active enzyme with functional overlap with BAM3. 2. Introducing bam1 into mex1 did not alter starch, maltose, or chlorophyll relative to mex1. 3. BAM1 therefore was not a major determinant of the mex1 phenotype under the tested conditions.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No additional phenotype described in mex1 background under tested conditions.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included experimentally as a comparator to BAM3 and BAM4; because its mutation did not alter the mex1 phenotype, its influence was limited under these conditions.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Probably lower priority target than BAM3/BAM4 for manipulating maltose accumulation in leaves.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mentioned as a comparative gene: loss of DPE2 causes very high maltose accumulation outside the chloroplast and may mildly decrease chlorophyll, but it was not experimentally analyzed in this paper.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Nighttime carbon intermediate exported from chloroplast starch breakdown
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cytosol and chloroplast-associated pool in leaves
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DPE2 was discussed as another gene affecting maltose metabolism. 2. dpe2 mutants reportedly accumulate about twice as much maltose as mex1. 3. It was not a primary experimental subject of the Results in this paper.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Background/comparative gene only; not a core Results gene by strict criteria, but included due to explicit gene–metabolite discussion in the paper.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucosyl units between malto-oligosaccharides in the chloroplast stroma, metabolizing maltotriose and related glucans during transitory starch breakdown and producing glucose-containing products.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Chloroplastic malto-oligosaccharide metabolism during nighttime starch breakdown
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Metabolizes starch-breakdown intermediates and prevents their accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Nighttime remobilized carbon from leaf transitory starch to support metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA mutant identification of dpe1-2; immunoblot showing loss of DPE1 protein; double- and triple-mutant analysis; starch and malto-oligosaccharide profiling; chlorophyll phenotyping; transmission electron microscopy
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of DPE1 in mex1 causes accumulation of maltotriose in addition to maltose and greatly enhances chlorosis and chloroplast degradation; loss of DPE1 alone causes maltotriose accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. dpe1 mutants accumulated high maltotriose. 2. The dpe1/mex1 double mutant accumulated both maltose and maltotriose and showed an extreme chlorotic dwarf phenotype with severe chloroplast degradation. 3. Introducing pgm into dpe1/mex1 rescued chlorosis, linking DPE1-dependent malto-oligosaccharide metabolism to the phenotype.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: In the mex1 background, severe growth retardation, failure to mature under 12-h photoperiod, and extreme chlorosis.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 12-h photoperiod; continuous low light improved growth; exogenous sugars improved growth but not chlorosis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A new dpe1-2 allele in Columbia background (GABI-KAT GK_339B11) contains a T-DNA insertion in the seventh intron and lacks detectable DPE1 protein. The severe double-mutant phenotype indicates that combined accumulation of maltose and maltotriose is associated with chloroplast degradation.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Avoid combining impaired stromal malto-oligosaccharide metabolism with blocked maltose export, as this destabilizes chloroplast homeostasis and growth.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Interconverts glucose-1-phosphate and glucose-6-phosphate in plastids to supply precursors for starch synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Precursor supply to chloroplastic starch synthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for starch synthesis, thereby enabling subsequent production of starch-breakdown products
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Nighttime remobilized carbon from leaf transitory starch to support metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic double- and triple-mutant analysis with mex1 and dpe1; iodine staining; malto-oligosaccharide measurements; chlorophyll phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Blocking starch synthesis via pgm prevents malto-oligosaccharide accumulation and rescues chlorosis in mex1 and dpe1/mex1 backgrounds.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. pgm plants were essentially starchless and contained very low malto-oligosaccharides. 2. mex1/pgm plants were not chlorotic, indicating that starch synthesis is required for the chlorotic phenotype of mex1. 3. The pgm mutation also rescued the severe dpe1/mex1 phenotype.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key upstream prerequisite for starch-derived malto-oligosaccharide accumulation in this study
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Growth retardation typical of pgm; starchless phenotype.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used genetically to demonstrate that starch synthesis is a prerequisite for accumulation of chlorosis-triggering starch breakdown intermediates.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Highlights that reducing transitory starch synthesis can suppress toxic accumulation of downstream starch-breakdown intermediates, although at the cost of starchless growth defects.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes α-1,4-glucan chains from nonreducing ends to release maltose during chloroplastic starch degradation.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Maltose-producing branch of chloroplastic starch breakdown
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major nocturnal starch breakdown product exported from chloroplast to support metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant combination analysis (bam3/mex1 and bam3/bam4/mex1); starch and maltose measurements; chlorophyll phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of BAM3 in mex1 decreases maltose accumulation, increases starch, and partially rescues chlorosis.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BAM3 is described as a key active enzyme in maltose production during starch breakdown. 2. Introducing bam3 into mex1 lowered nighttime maltose and increased starch. 3. Reduced maltose accumulation coincided with increased chlorophyll content, supporting a causal link between maltose accumulation and chlorosis.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key active enzyme for maltose production in the pathway
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: bam3 mutation causes starch-excess phenotype and impaired starch breakdown.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 12-h photoperiod; measurements at end of day and 4 h into night
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Single bam3 mutants reduce nocturnal maltose and starch breakdown and show a starch-excess phenotype.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Partial reduction of BAM3 activity could lower toxic chloroplastic maltose accumulation where export is limiting, but may impair normal nighttime starch mobilization.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Functions in regulation of starch degradation leading to maltose production; not assigned here as a clearly active catalytic beta-amylase in this paper.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Regulatory component of the maltose-producing branch of starch breakdown
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Promotes formation of starch-breakdown products including maltose
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major nocturnal starch breakdown product exported from chloroplast to support metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA mutant combination analysis (bam4/mex1 and bam3/bam4/mex1); starch and maltose measurements; chlorophyll phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of BAM4 in mex1 decreases maltose accumulation, increases starch, and partially rescues chlorosis; effects are stronger with bam3/bam4/mex1.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BAM4 is described as being involved in regulation of starch degradation. 2. Introducing bam4 into mex1 reduced maltose and increased chlorophyll compared with mex1. 3. Combined removal of BAM3 and BAM4 enhanced suppression of the mex1 phenotype.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Contributes to pathway control but not established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: bam4 mutation causes starch-excess phenotype and reduced starch breakdown.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 12-h photoperiod; measurements at end of day and 4 h into night
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper states BAM4 is thought to regulate starch degradation rather than acting as the key active enzyme.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Modulating BAM4 may buffer maltose accumulation when maltose export is compromised, but normal nocturnal carbon mobilization may be reduced.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Phosphorylates amylopectin at the starch granule surface to enable subsequent starch degradation.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Enables precursor mobilization toward maltose production
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major nocturnal starch breakdown product exported from chloroplast to support metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant combination analysis (gwd/sex1-3 with mex1); immunoblot genotyping for GWD; starch and maltose measurements; chlorophyll phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of GWD in mex1 increases starch, decreases maltose, and alleviates chlorosis, showing that blocking starch breakdown upstream of maltose production suppresses the phenotype.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GWD acts at the start of starch breakdown by phosphorylating amylopectin. 2. gwd/mex1 mutants had increased starch, decreased maltose, and increased chlorophyll relative to mex1. 3. These data support the conclusion that production of starch-breakdown intermediates triggers chlorosis in mex1.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key initiating step for starch breakdown
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: gwd mutants are starch-excess and experience nighttime carbon starvation symptoms in other contexts.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 12-h photoperiod; measurements at end of day and 4 h into night
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Referred to as sex1-3 in this study.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Upstream suppression of starch breakdown can protect chloroplasts from toxic maltose buildup when export is impaired, but reduces normal starch remobilization.
